past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 8 years ago, well-controlled on medication
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 5 years ago, managed with metformin, HbA1c 6.8%
Mild bilateral cataracts, stable on last ophthalmology exam 6 months ago
History of migraines with visual aura in her 30s and 40s, resolved
family_history: Father died at age 81, had progressive cognitive decline in his late 70s, never formally diagnosed
Mother died at age 79 of breast cancer, cognitively intact
One sister, age 59, recently diagnosed with depression
No known family history of early-onset dementia or movement disorders

social_history.living_situation: Lives with her husband in a two-story house. Two adult children living out of state
social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, 1-2 glasses of wine per week
social_history.exercise: Used to swim 3 times per week, has reduced to once weekly over the past year due to difficulty navigating the pool facility

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, date, and situation. MMSE 26/30, losing points on serial sevens (2 errors) and pentagons copy (unable to reproduce interlocking pentagons accurately). Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) 23/30 with particular difficulty on visuospatial/executive tasks (cube copy distorted, clock drawing with misplaced numbers clustered to right side, trail-making B errors). Delayed recall 4/5 words (retrieved the 5th with categorical cue). Naming intact. Repetition intact. Verbal fluency mildly reduced (12 animals in 60 seconds). Reading: occasional letter substitution errors. Writing: intact for dictated sentences.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light. Extraocular movements full, no nystagmus. Visual fields full to confrontation testing with standard finger counting; however, simultaneous bilateral stimulation reveals left visual extinction on 3 of 5 trials. Face symmetric. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk and tone normal throughout all extremities. Strength 5/5 in all muscle groups. No pronator drift. No tremor, rigidity, or involuntary movements.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact throughout. Proprioception intact at great toes bilaterally. Vibration mildly reduced at bilateral medial malleoli (attributed to diabetic neuropathy). Graphesthesia impaired in bilateral hands (unable to identify 3 of 6 numbers traced on palm). Stereognosis impaired in left hand (unable to identify key, correctly identified coin and paperclip).
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric in biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No frontal release signs.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally. Heel-to-shin intact. Rapid alternating movements intact. However, patient demonstrates significant difficulty with sequential finger tapping tasks and reproducing hand sequences (Luria test).
neurological_exam.gait: Steady, normal base and stride. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative. No parkinsonian features.
neurological_exam.additional: Optic ataxia noted on reaching tasks: patient misreaches for objects placed in peripheral visual fields, particularly on the left side. Difficulty with visually guided hand movements despite intact primary motor and sensory function. No paratonic rigidity. No myoclonus. No grasp reflex.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive difficulty reading and problems with spatial navigation over the past 18 months
patient.history_present_illness: Dr. Yuki Nakamura is a 65-year-old right-handed architect who presents to the neurology outpatient clinic accompanied by her husband, James, for evaluation of progressive difficulty with visual and spatial tasks over the past eighteen months. Her husband reports that the first noticeable change occurred approximately eighteen months ago when she began struggling to read architectural blueprints at work, a task she had performed with ease for over thirty years. She initially attributed this to worsening vision and saw her ophthalmologist, who noted only mild, stable bilateral cataracts insufficient to explain her symptoms. Her corrected visual acuity was measured at 20/25 bilaterally at that time. Over the following months, she developed increasing difficulty reading text, frequently losing her place on the page and misidentifying letters, which she described as the words seeming to 'jump around.' She has had multiple near-miss incidents while driving, primarily involving misjudging distances when parking or changing lanes, and she voluntarily stopped driving six months ago after sideswiping a parked car. At her architecture firm, colleagues noted she was making errors in spatial layout and proportion that were uncharacteristic of her meticulous work, and she has had difficulty using computer-aided design software that she previously used expertly. She has become noticeably clumsy, frequently knocking over glasses and reaching past objects she is trying to grasp. Her husband notes she occasionally walks into door frames on her left side. She recently became lost in a shopping mall she has visited for over twenty years, unable to orient herself to find the exit. Remarkably, her memory for conversations and events remains quite good. She accurately recalled details of a family dinner from the previous weekend, including who attended and what was discussed. She continues to manage her medications, pay bills, and maintain her social calendar without difficulty. She remains articulate and engages warmly in conversation. There have been no personality changes, behavioral disturbances, hallucinations, or mood symptoms beyond understandable frustration with her visual difficulties. She denies headaches, seizures, tremor, weakness, numbness, or difficulty walking. Her husband has not observed any features suggestive of REM sleep behavior disorder. She has had no episodes of fluctuating alertness or confusion.

signal_characteristics.T1: Prominent sulcal widening in bilateral parieto-occipital regions, more marked on the right
signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: No signal abnormality in the atrophic regions
Confluent periventricular caps and rims with scattered deep white matter punctate foci, bilateral
signal_characteristics.T1: Corresponding subtle T1 hypointensities in confluent areas

signal_characteristics.T1_coronal: Hippocampal volumes appear within normal limits for age (Scheltens grade 0-1)

signal_characteristics.T1: Age-appropriate cortical volume without focal atrophy

volumetrics.hippocampal_volume: Within normal limits for age (25th-50th percentile)
volumetrics.parietal_cortical_thickness: Reduced bilaterally, right > left, below 5th percentile for age
volumetrics.occipital_cortical_thickness: Reduced bilaterally, below 10th percentile for age

additional_observations: No acute infarcts on DWI sequences
No mass lesions or pathological enhancement post-gadolinium
No evidence of cerebral microbleeds on SWI
Moderate small vessel disease burden given age and vascular risk factors
mri.impression: Posterior-predominant cortical atrophy involving bilateral parieto-occipital regions, right greater than left, with relative preservation of hippocampal volumes and anterior temporal structures. Moderate white matter disease of presumed vascular etiology (Fazekas grade 2) noted in the setting of hypertension and diabetes. The pattern of posterior cortical atrophy is atypical for Alzheimer's disease but could represent posterior cortical atrophy variant. Differential considerations include vascular cognitive impairment given the white matter burden, or a primary neurodegenerative process with posterior predominance. Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Sodium 152 mEq/L (H), Glucose 121 mg/dL (H), HbA1c 6.4 % (H).

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: WAIS-IV Perceptual Reasoning Index 76 standard score (L), WAIS-IV Working Memory Index 88 standard score (L), WAIS-IV Processing Speed Index 75 standard score (L), Rey Complex Figure Copy 17 raw (max 36) (L), Rey Complex Figure Delayed Recall 9 raw (max 36) (L), Trail Making Test B 132 seconds (H), Judgment of Line Orientation 12 raw (max 30) (L), Hooper Visual Organization Test 15.4 raw (max 30) (L).
abnormal_values_summary: WAIS-IV Perceptual Reasoning Index: 76 standard score (H)
WAIS-IV Working Memory Index: 88 standard score) (H)
WAIS-IV Processing Speed Index: 75 standard score (H)
Rey Complex Figure Delayed Recall: 9 raw (max 36) (H)

signal_characteristics.regional_pattern: Diffuse cortical amyloid deposition with highest uptake in precuneus, posterior cingulate, and lateral parietal cortex

output.impression: POSITIVE amyloid PET scan demonstrating diffuse cortical amyloid-beta deposition. Clinical correlation recommended.

signal_characteristics.pattern: Marked hypometabolism in bilateral parieto-occipital regions, right > left, extending into posterior temporal cortex

additional_observations: Relative preservation of frontal and medial temporal metabolism
Primary visual cortex less severely affected than association visual cortex
Posterior cingulate and precuneus show moderate hypometabolism
output.impression: FDG-PET demonstrates a posterior-predominant hypometabolic pattern involving bilateral parieto-occipital cortex with relative sparing of frontal and medial temporal regions. Clinical correlation recommended.

special_tests.Abeta42: 480 pg/mL (borderline low; cutoff <500 pg/mL suggestive of amyloid pathology)

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 14 cmH2O. WBC: 1 cell/uL (normal) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 39.7 mg/dL. Glucose: 65.1 mg/dL (ratio 0.53 (serum glucose 118 mg/dL)). Abeta42: 480 pg/mL (borderline low; cutoff <500 pg/mL suggestive of amyloid pathology). Abeta42_40_ratio: 0.062 (decreased; normal >0.089; suggestive of amyloid pathology). Phospho-tau_181: 48 pg/mL (borderline elevated; cutoff >40 pg/mL). Total_tau: 320 pg/mL (mildly elevated; cutoff >300 pg/mL). NfL: 1250 pg/mL (mildly elevated, suggestive of neurodegeneration). 14-3-3_protein: Negative. RT-QuIC: Negative.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves and sleep spindles captured in brief drowsiness

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: No change in background or activation of epileptiform discharges

output.impression: Normal awake and drowsy EEG. The posterior dominant rhythm is well-formed at 9 Hz, which is within normal limits. No epileptiform discharges. No focal slowing. This normal EEG does not exclude a neurodegenerative process, as EEG changes in posterior cortical atrophy and early Alzheimer's disease may not manifest until later stages.
output.limitations: Brief recording (30 minutes); sleep not fully captured

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Simultanagnosia testing Present  (H), Optic ataxia Present  (H), Oculomotor apraxia Mild  (H).

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Early-stage Alzheimer's disease, posterior cortical atrophy variant (Benson syndrome)

differential.diagnosis: Posterior cortical atrophy due to Alzheimer's disease
differential.key_features: Progressive visuospatial dysfunction with preserved episodic memory, posterior cortical atrophy on MRI, positive amyloid PET, compatible CSF biomarkers, APOE e4 carrier

Significant white matter disease on MRI (Fazekas 2), vascular risk factors (hypertension, diabetes), but the posterior-predominant visuospatial profile does not match typical vascular cognitive impairment pattern
Can present with visuospatial deficits, but patient lacks visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, REM sleep behavior disorder, and cognitive fluctuations
Can cause posterior cortical dysfunction, but patient lacks asymmetric rigidity, dystonia, alien limb phenomenon, or myoclonus
Heidenhain variant can present with visual symptoms, but the slowly progressive course (18 months without rapid decline), normal EEG, negative 14-3-3 and RT-QuIC effectively exclude this

optimal_actions.action: Perform detailed neurological examination with emphasis on higher cortical visual functions, including tests for simultanagnosia, optic ataxia, and oculomotor apraxia
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Balint syndrome components: simultanagnosia, optic ataxia, oculomotor apraxia; visual extinction on double simultaneous stimulation; impaired graphesthesia and stereognosis

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with volumetric sequences to characterize pattern of atrophy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Posterior-predominant parieto-occipital atrophy with relatively preserved hippocampal volumes

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory work-up to exclude reversible causes of cognitive impairment (CBC, BMP, TSH, B12, folate, RPR, HIV)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal results except for known diabetes; no reversible causes identified

optimal_actions.action: Request comprehensive neuropsychological testing to characterize the cognitive profile
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Severely impaired visuospatial and visuoconstructional abilities with relatively preserved episodic memory and language

optimal_actions.action: Refer for neuro-ophthalmology consultation to differentiate cortical visual dysfunction from primary ophthalmologic disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Preserved primary visual functions with impaired higher-order visual processing (Balint syndrome)

optimal_actions.action: Order amyloid PET scan to confirm underlying Alzheimer's pathology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive amyloid PET with diffuse cortical amyloid deposition

optimal_actions.action: Consider FDG-PET to characterize metabolic pattern
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Posterior-predominant hypometabolism in parieto-occipital regions with preserved frontal and medial temporal metabolism

optimal_actions.action: Consider CSF biomarkers (Abeta42, phospho-tau, total tau) if amyloid PET unavailable or equivocal
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Borderline low Abeta42, decreased Abeta42/40 ratio, borderline elevated phospho-tau and total tau

optimal_actions.action: Consider APOE genotyping and early-onset AD gene panel given age <65 at symptom onset
optimal_actions.expected_finding: APOE e3/e4 heterozygous carrier; no autosomal dominant AD mutations

optimal_actions.action: Initiate cholinesterase inhibitor therapy (donepezil 5 mg daily) and discuss diagnosis, prognosis, and support resources with patient and family

critical_actions: Recognize that the visual and spatial symptoms are cortical in origin, not ophthalmologic
Order brain MRI to identify the posterior-predominant pattern of atrophy
Obtain neuropsychological testing to demonstrate the dissociation between preserved memory and impaired visuospatial function
Pursue amyloid biomarkers (PET or CSF) to confirm underlying Alzheimer's pathology
Exclude reversible causes of cognitive decline with appropriate laboratory testing
Do not dismiss symptoms as solely due to cataracts or normal aging
contraindicated_actions: Attributing all symptoms to cataracts or refractive error and deferring neurological evaluation
Diagnosing typical Alzheimer's disease and expecting a memory-predominant profile
Prescribing anticholinergic medications which would worsen cognitive function
Performing brain biopsy when non-invasive biomarkers are available
Ordering invasive cerebral angiography without indication
Initiating memantine as monotherapy in early-stage disease without cholinesterase inhibitor
key_reasoning_points: The presentation of progressive visuospatial dysfunction with preserved episodic memory is the hallmark of posterior cortical atrophy (PCA), not typical Alzheimer's disease
PCA is most commonly caused by Alzheimer's pathology (~80% of cases) and is classified as an atypical AD variant
The relatively preserved hippocampal volumes on MRI with posterior-predominant atrophy distinguish PCA from typical AD

The moderate white matter disease is a red herring that may initially suggest vascular cognitive impairment, but the clinical profile does not match the typical executive dysfunction pattern of vascular dementia
The normal EEG is expected in early PCA and does not exclude the diagnosis
Components of Balint syndrome (simultanagnosia, optic ataxia, oculomotor apraxia) localize the dysfunction to bilateral parieto-occipital cortex
Amyloid PET is the most definitive non-invasive test to confirm underlying AD pathology in atypical presentations
Age at symptom onset (~63-64) qualifies as early-onset AD, which has a higher prevalence of atypical variants like PCA
The equivocal CSF biomarkers illustrate why amyloid PET may be preferred in diagnostically challenging cases

metadata.clinical_notes: Diagnostic puzzle case featuring posterior cortical atrophy (Benson syndrome) as an atypical variant of early Alzheimer's disease. Key challenges include the non-amnestic presentation, red herring of white matter disease suggesting vascular etiology, stable cataracts as a potential misattribution target, and equivocal CSF biomarkers.
teaching_points: PCA presents with visuospatial rather than memory deficits
Normal hippocampal volumes do not exclude Alzheimer's disease
Amyloid biomarkers are essential for confirming AD pathology in atypical presentations
White matter disease burden should not automatically lead to a vascular dementia diagnosis
